"How are you going to build a relationship with someone you described in the past as a racist, misogynistic, self-confessed groper?" Kay asked during the Nov. 12, 2024, interview.

"I’ve said some things in the past," Miliband responded. "My job now as a government minister is to work with people."

The differences extend beyond Miliband’s rhetoric about Trump. As energy secretary, Miliband became the public face of the Labour government’s net-zero agenda and its opposition to issuing new licenses for North Sea oil and gas exploration. Trump and members of his administration have pushed Britain to increase domestic fossil-fuel production.

Nile Gardiner, director of the Margaret Thatcher Center for Freedom at the Heritage Foundation and a former foreign policy aide to Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher, told Fox News Digital Miliband’s appointment was a "disastrous" decision.

"I think the appointment of Ed Miliband as U.K. foreign secretary is disastrous for Britain," Gardiner said in an interview Tuesday. "It’s a demonstration of a real lack of strategic thinking on the part of Andy Burnham.

"Ed Miliband is no friend of the United States," Gardiner continued. "He has been an outspoken critic of President Trump. Miliband is also widely viewed by U.S. conservatives as the architect of the whole net-zero agenda, which is viewed as completely poisonous by the U.S. administration."

EUROPE'S 'GREEN IDEOLOGY' CLIMATE AGENDA RISKS CRUSHING ITS INDUSTRY, ENVIRONMENT MINISTER WARNS

Gardiner called the appointment a "very high-risk move" that "makes no sense."

"Burnham is already off to a very bad start with this appointment," he said. "I’m in no doubt there will be very significant tensions between President Trump and Prime Minister Burnham, and Miliband’s appointment is quite possibly the worst appointment that Burnham could make as foreign secretary."

As he delivered his first remarks following the appointment, Miliband mentioned his family’s experience as Holocaust survivors.

"It’s the honor of my life, being asked by the prime minister to be the foreign secretary of our country," Miliband said in a statement Tuesday. "My parents came here, Jewish refugees fleeing from the Nazis and, for them, Britain provided sanctuary and a beacon of hope in the fight against fascism.

"At a time when the values of democracy, the rule of law and freedom are under threat as never before, I will carry that spirit, their spirit, into my work as foreign secretary," he said. "And now I’m going to go and get on with the task."

Miliband also pledged to nurture Britain’s "strong, enduring and essential alliance" with the United States while deepening its strategic relationship with the European Union, according to the Financial Times.

His appointment has also raised concerns among some British Jewish and pro-Israel commentators.

Stephen Pollard, a former editor and current columnist for The Jewish Chronicle, argued in an opinion piece published Tuesday that Miliband’s record as Labour leader, including changes to party leadership rules that preceded Jeremy Corbyn’s rise, should concern British Jews and supporters of Israel. Pollard predicted that the Burnham government would pursue additional sanctions against Israel with the support of a Jewish foreign secretary.

The Burnham government had not announced additional sanctions against Israel as of Tuesday.

Conservative Member of Parliament Priti Patel separately criticized Burnham and Miliband over plans to proceed with the Chagos Islands agreement.

"Burnham and Miliband are wrong to press ahead with Labour’s appalling Chagos Surrender," Patel wrote on X, arguing that the agreement would transfer British territory and cost taxpayers £35 billion (approximately $47 billion.)

"Once again, the same old Labour Party is undermining our national security and making Britain weaker on the international stage," she added.

On Monday, President Donald Trump said he spoke with Burnham shortly after he took office, calling the conversation "very good." Trump added that he and Burnham plan to meet in the "not too distant future" to discuss issues of mutual interest.

The White House, State Department, Miliband’s office and Burnham’s office did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital’s requests for comment.

Fox News' Bonny Chu contributed to this article.

item.title: Park visitors face flood danger out West while severe storms rip through NY, NJ
item.description: Flash flood warnings remain active across Utah and Colorado, disrupting vacations during peak travel. Parts of New York and New Jersey were also pummeled by relentless rain July 21.
item.content:encoded:
Life-threatening flash flooding threatened both the East and West coasts Tuesday, as severe storms inundated parts of the Northeast while monsoon rains continued to drench the Southwest — prompting warnings across Utah and Colorado and disrupting travel.

The National Weather Service (NWS) issued flood watches for parts of New York, New Jersey and Connecticut on Tuesday, along with tornado watches covering portions of the region through the evening as strong thunderstorms moved through the area.

The NWS also issued a flood watch for much of southern and central Utah, warning of possible flash flooding in Zion National Park, Capitol Reef National Park, Glen Canyon National Recreation Area and parts of Canyonlands.

ONE OF AMERICA'S MOST BEAUTIFUL NATIONAL PARKS NOW FACING DISNEYLAND-LEVEL CROWDS

The weather service warned of "life-threatening flash flooding" and said excessive rainfall over the burn scar could trigger debris flows carrying "rock, mud, vegetation and other loose materials."

"Excessive rainfall may result in flooding of slot canyons, normally dry washes, slick rock areas, recent burn scars including Forsyth, France Canyon, Cottonwood, Silver King and Monroe Canyon, and other low-lying and flood-prone locations," the notice said. "Flooding may occur in poor drainage and urban areas."

The National Park Service told Fox News Digital that flash floods can be "unpredictable and deadly, particularly in the West because of the arid environment that cannot rapidly absorb water."

It added, "The National Park Service wants visitors to have a memorable national park experience and get home safely and without injury. Visitor safety is our top priority, and we urge the public to stay aware of changing weather conditions."

As monsoon storms continued Monday, the NWS also issued multiple flash flood warnings across western Colorado, including one that urged residents and visitors to move away from the Turner Gulch burn scar.

Photos released by Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park on Friday show the aftermath of mudslides after heavy monsoon rains triggered flash flooding in the western Colorado park.

More than 20 day-trippers, campers and anglers were temporarily stranded at Gunnison National Park after mudslides blocked East Portal Road, officials added. No injuries were reported.

YELLOWSTONE BISON ATTACK WITNESS BELIEVES MAN SAVED HIS GRANDSON BY TAKING BRUNT OF ATTACK

Campers and hikers on forums dedicated to national parks have also been discussing the dangers of monsoon season in the Southwest.

On a forum dedicated to Zion National Park, one visitor who shared video of heavy rain at the park called it "one of the most beautiful and shocking displays of nature's power" they had ever seen.

Others stressed the dangers of hiking during monsoon season.

"Those flash flood alerts are no joke. We got caught in a slot canyon once and the water rose so freaking fast," one person wrote.

"Yeah, be careful with flash flooding out there when the rain comes," another added.

GRAND CANYON MYSTERY ILLNESS REPORTEDLY TRIGGERS NPS INVESTIGATION AFTER RAFTERS REPORT ALARMING SYMPTOMS

The ongoing flood threat comes after a Utah family's hiking trip turned deadly when flash floodwaters swept through the area.

According to the Wayne County Sheriff's Office, the family arrived at the Sunglow Campground area, part of Fishlake National Forest near Bicknell, Utah, on Friday morning before setting out to hike and canyoneer.

Search crews later found the family's bodies after floodwaters swept through the area.

"It is believed the family fell victim to flooding caused by heavy rains," the sheriff’s office said.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER

The victims were later identified as Provo Fire Capt. Spencer Long, his wife, Katrina, and their three sons, Reid, Thayne and Gage.

The couple's daughter, who was not on the trip, survived.

The U.S. Forest Service told Fox News Digital that it was "deeply saddened by the tragic loss of life resulting from the apparent flash flood near the Sunglow Campground area on the Fishlake National Forest."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE LIFESTYLE NEWS

The safety of visitors and employees remains our highest priority," the agency said. 

"We encourage everyone recreating on public lands to monitor current weather forecasts, heed flash flood warnings, and avoid entering narrow canyons, washes, or other flood-prone areas when storms are possible."

The campground sits in a red rock canyon near Capitol Reef National Park and is popular with hikers and canyoneers.

The Associated Press contributed reporting.

item.title: Trump orders US airlines to resume direct flights to Lebanon after White House meeting with president
item.description: President Trump called Lebanon "a country of great intellect" and said his administration has done things regarding Hezbollah that the world will notice.
item.content:encoded:
President Donald Trump on Tuesday announced he is directing his administration to allow all U.S. airlines to fly to Lebanon after a White House meeting with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun, saying the move will make it easier for Americans to visit the country.

In a Truth Social post after the meeting, Trump praised Aoun for "working to transform his Country" and said he was directing his administration to allow all U.S. airline carriers to "fly directly to Lebanon so that Americans can easily visit this beautiful land."

"After meeting with the President of Lebanon, Joseph Aoun, who has done a remarkable job working to transform his Country, I am hereby directing my Administration to allow all U.S. airline carriers to fly directly to Lebanon so that Americans can easily visit this beautiful land," Trump wrote. "Hopefully, other Countries will do the same. Enjoy!"

IRAN-BACKED PROXIES SABOTAGING LEBANON PEACE AS JD VANCE LEADS US DELEGATION TO SWITZERLAND, HERZOG SAYS

Speaking alongside Aoun in the Oval Office earlier Tuesday, Trump called Lebanon "a country of great intellect" and vowed the U.S. would "help it a lot," while hinting at additional moves beyond his announcement on direct flights. 

"We've already solved some of them for Lebanon," Trump said. "As you know, there's a Hezbollah problem. But we've done some things that I think the world will take notice."

The announcement marks another step in the Trump administration's efforts to strengthen ties with Lebanon after months of U.S.-backed diplomacy aimed at reducing Hezbollah's influence and increasing regional stability.

Secretary of Transportation Sean Duffy took to social media after the president's announcement, writing on X, "You got it, Mr. President."

The announcement comes after months of U.S.-backed diplomacy between Lebanon and Israel as the Trump administration has sought to reduce Hezbollah's influence and bolster regional stability.

ISRAEL–HEZBOLLAH CEASEFIRE BECOMES FIRST TEST OF TRUMP IRAN FRAMEWORK AFTER TALKS DELAY

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu called the agreement "a severe blow to Iran," while U.S. Ambassador to Israel Mike Huckabee described it as a "historic opportunity," adding that disarming Hezbollah remained essential to any lasting peace.

Aoun thanked the Trump administration for helping facilitate negotiations and supporting Lebanon during the talks.

The White House did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital's request for additional comment, including on the timeline for implementing the policy.

Fox News Digital's Benjamin Weinthal contributed to this reporting.

item.title: Ella Langley joins music royalty with 'insane' milestone for 'Choosin' Texas'
item.description: Ella Langley's "Choosin' Texas" tied a Billboard Hot 100 record with 14 weeks at No. 1, matching Mariah Carey and Whitney Houston among non-holiday titles by women.
item.content:encoded:
Ella Langley likely has a smile written on her face.

Her "Choosin' Texas" anthem placed at No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 100 charts, its 14th week at the top, tying a record with two iconic hits.

Langley, 27, called the career milestone "insane" in a post shared on Instagram stories shortly after earning the spot.

ELLA LANGLEY SUFFERS WARDROBE MALFUNCTION WHILE OPENING FOR MORGAN WALLEN IN BALTIMORE

The country ballad first topped the Hot 100 in February and now matches Mariah Carey's "We Belong Together" and Whitney Houston's "I Will Always Love You" for most weeks at No. 1 among non-holiday titles by women with no male-billed acts.

In addition, "Choosing Texas" ties Carey's "All I Want for Christmas Is You" for second-longest ranking hit.

Langley broke onto the country music scene nearly a decade ago and released her debut studio album, "Hungover," in 2024.

MORGAN WALLEN AND ELLA LANGLEY HOLD THE TOP TWO ALBUM SPOTS IN AMERICA FOR FIRST HALF OF 2026

The catalog brought her immediate accolades with hits, including "You Look Like You Love Me" and "Weren't for the Wind" both reaching No. 1 on country charts.

Her sophomore release, "Dandelion," debuted at No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 200 upon its release in April.

"Choosin' Texas" not only capped the Billboard Hot 100, but recently earned Langley the title of first female artist to simultaneously top the Hot 100, Hot Country Songs and Country Airplay charts.

MIRANDA LAMBERT DELIVERS STRAIGHT-SHOOTING ADVICE TO RISING COUNTRY STAR IN SURPRISE BATHROOM ENCOUNTER

The "Dandelion" title was inspired by the sturdy plant which is known to withstand challenging environments.

"I learned that dandelion tea is actually a natural detox for the liver," Ella told Billboard ahead of the release. "So, a record called ‘Dandelion’ after a record called hungover made all the sense in the world to me.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

"I’ve never poured more of myself into a project, into a song, into an idea, and it’s fallen out so beautifully. It’s about learning yourself, making mistakes and realizing that it’s all just part of life.

"I want this record to be something that fans can relate to but also something they want to get up and dance to at the same time. To me, this record feels like fireflies in the summertime. It feels like windows down on a red dirt road. It feels like the best kind of Sunday afternoon. It feels like those days I remember as a kid where it felt like there weren’t enough hours in the day. This record is really a love letter to that."

Langley served as executive producer on the album and co-wrote "Choosin' Texas" alongside Miranda Lambert, Luke Dick and JoyBeth Taylor.

item.title: Woman attacked by brown bear while hiking with friend and 3 dogs near Alaska state park
item.description: A woman was injured after a brown bear attacked her on an overgrown trail area near Chugach State Park before rescuers safely evacuated her, police said.
item.content:encoded:
A woman was attacked by a brown bear Saturday while hiking with a friend and three dogs near a state park in Alaska, authorities said.

The woman sustained non-life-threatening injuries and is expected to recover, the Anchorage Police Department (APD) said Sunday.

The attack happened late afternoon near the Bear Valley residential neighborhood, adjacent to Chugach State Park in south Anchorage, the Alaska Department of Fish and Game (ADF&G) said in a separate statement on Monday.

TERRIFIED HIKERS FACE NIGHTMARE SURVIVAL SCENARIO AFTER HEART-POUNDING GRIZZLY BEAR ENCOUNTER

Officials said the hikers were on an unofficial, user-made trail that climbs a steep hillside in a zigzag pattern. The area was heavily overgrown with dense vegetation, which created poor visibility and increased the risk of surprising wildlife, ADF&G said.

"The bear came out of the dense alders, made brief contact with one of the hikers, and left the scene," ADF&G said.

One of the victim's dogs was also off leash during the encounter and became separated from the group, according to the authorities. Another group of hikers later found the "uninjured dog and brought it back to safety, ultimately returning it to its home," ADF&G said.

HIDDEN DANGER AT POPULAR US NATIONAL PARK FORCES TRAIL CLOSURES AS BEAR SIGHTINGS CLIMB

According to wildlife officials, bears are known to cache and defend food sources this time of year and noted reports that at least one moose had recently been killed nearby.

"High winds and inclement weather conditions at the time of the encounter may have increased the chance that the bear was surprised by the hikers and their dogs, even if they were making noise," ADF&G said.

BEAR ATTACK IN YELLOWSTONE NATIONAL PARK LEAVES 2 HIKERS INJURED

"There are many private and public trails throughout Anchorage and the surrounding area that are overgrown at this time of year with tall grass and leafed out shrubs, which can greatly increase the chances of surprise encounters with wildlife."

First responders from multiple agencies, including the Anchorage Fire Department, Alaska Wildlife Troopers and Alaska State Troopers, responded.

Upon arrival, police officers reportedly hiked up the trail to locate the injured adult.

Firefighters then carried the injured woman down the trail before transporting her to a hospital.

During the rescue, APD officers deployed a drone and located a brown bear nearby. Officers continued monitoring the bear throughout the rescue, allowing first responders to safely treat and evacuate the injured hiker.
